unit.name: Phrase structure and dependency structure as views of syntax
unit.description: Introduces the lecture, connects it to Assignment 2, reviews constituency/phrase-structure grammar, and contrasts it with dependency grammar as a head-dependent representation.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by framing dependency parsing as the core task behind the second half of Assignment 2 and noting that students will build the decision-making component of a neural dependency parser in PyTorch [ts=64s] [ts=72s]. It then reviews phrase structure grammar: words belong to parts of speech such as nouns, adjectives, determiners, and prepositions, and these combine into larger constituents like noun phrases and prepositional phrases [ts=149s] [ts=304s]. The instructor gives CFG-style rewrite rules for noun phrases, including determiner plus noun, optional adjectives, repeated adjectives, and noun phrases followed by prepositional phrases [ts=367s] [ts=423s]. He then contrasts this with dependency structure, where the sentence is organized around head words and their modifiers or arguments, illustrated with look in the large crate in the kitchen by the door [ts=604s] [ts=676s]. The segment closes by arguing that humans and NLP systems must recover which words modify which others in order to understand meaning, and foreshadows that transformers later encode similar structure implicitly even without explicit parses [ts=759s] [ts=868s].

key_points.text: Phrase structure grammar models syntax with parts of speech and larger constituents such as noun phrases and prepositional phrases.
A noun phrase can be described with rules like determiner plus adjective-star plus noun, optionally followed by a prepositional phrase.
Dependency structure represents a sentence through head words and the words that modify or depend on them.
Understanding language requires recovering what modifies what, not just reading a linear sequence of words or sounds.

unit_summary_for_generation: The lecture opens by framing dependency parsing as the core task behind the second half of Assignment 2 and noting that students will build the decision-making component of a neural dependency parser in PyTorch [ts=64s] [ts=72s]. It then reviews phrase structure grammar: words belong to parts of speech such as nouns, adjectives, determiners, and prepositions, and these combine into larger constituents like noun phrases and prepositional phrases [ts=149s] [ts=304s]. The instructor gives CFG-style rewrite rules for noun phrases, including determiner plus noun, optional adjectives, repeated adjectives, and noun phrases followed by prepositional phrases [ts=367s] [ts=423s]. He then contrasts this with dependency structure, where the sentence is organized around head words and their modifiers or arguments, illustrated with look in the large crate in the kitchen by the door [ts=604s] [ts=676s]. The segment closes by arguing that humans and NLP systems must recover which words modify which others in order to understand meaning, and foreshadows that transformers later encode similar structure implicitly even without explicit parses [ts=759s] [ts=868s].

kp_catalog.name: Phrase structure grammar
kp_catalog.description: A constituency-based view of syntax in which parts of speech combine into hierarchical phrases through rewrite rules such as noun phrase and verb phrase rules.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because phrase structure grammar clarifies a constituency-based view of syntax in which parts of speech combine into hierarchical phrases through rewrite rules such as noun phrase and verb phrase rules, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Dependency grammar
kp_catalog.description: A syntactic representation built from head-dependent relations, where each word is linked to the word it modifies or depends on.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dependency grammar formalizes how a syntactic representation built from head-dependent relations, where each word is linked to the word it modifies or depends on, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
